title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] ADITI ROY: Hello, and welcome. My name is Aditi Roy, longtime
business and news reporter and founder of Aditi Roy Media. Today I'm thrilled to
bring our next speaker on to the stage, Waymo
Co-CEO Dmitri Dolgov. Dimitri was one of the
founders of Google's original self-driving
car project. And he's going to take us
through an adventurous ride through the history
of autonomous driving and where it will
lead in the future. Please join me in
welcoming Dmitri. [APPLAUSE] DMITRI DOLGOV: Thank you. Hi, everyone. Good afternoon. It's great to be here. Most of the AI that
people talk about nowadays lives in the digital world, and
it is incredibly impressive. It can write code. It can diagnose
illness, compose music, create videos, and so much more. But the AI that we
are building at Waymo doesn't live on your screen. It lives in real
life, among people, in weather, in
traffic, in motion. So today we'll talk about the
most advanced application of AI in the physical world,
Waymo's autonomous vehicles. But why do we need autonomous
vehicles in the first place? Well, because when it
comes to road safety, the status quo is unacceptable. Every year, more
than a million people lose their lives to road
collisions across the world. To break that down, every
26 seconds, a person loses their life to
a road collision. This is staggering. And the vast majority
of these tragedies are due to human error. We can do better. And this is why we founded
Waymo more than 15 years ago, to challenge the status quo
and to build a safer mobility option for all. Let's go back to those early
days for just a minute. It's quite fitting that we
are here today at Google I/O because we started in 2009 as
the Google self-driving car project. In fact, this very
parking lot, where we're sitting right now next
to the Shoreline amphitheater, is where, in 2009, we did
our very first car bring up and testing, as well as an
occasional autonomous race. So check this out and see if
you can spot the amphitheater in the background. [VIDEO PLAYBACK]
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
we're sitting right now next
to the Shoreline amphitheater, is where, in 2009, we did
our very first car bring up and testing, as well as an
occasional autonomous race. So check this out and see if
you can spot the amphitheater in the background. [VIDEO PLAYBACK] [MUSIC PLAYING] [END PLAYBACK] I honestly did not know that you
could drift a Prius until then. And by the way,
those cones that you see lying down on
the ground, they're supposed to be like that. We did not hit them. They're pointer cones. They tell you which way
you're supposed to go, and we use them to
set up the course to allow human drivers to
compete against our autonomous racing Prius. When we started in 2009,
we had a pretty small team. It was about a dozen engineers
who came together with one goal, to prove that autonomous
vehicles were possible. Here are some pictures of the
team from those early days. There's a younger version of me. I practically lived in the
car for the first year or so, and nowadays we hear from our
Waymo riders that many of them like to use Waymo as
their mobile workspace. So I like to take credit
for actually starting that trend back in 2009. Many of us, at the time, came
from the so-called DARPA Grand Challenges. Those were competitions,
in early 2000s, that really pushed forward the
state of the art in robotics and autonomy. So that meant that
many of us had some experience in the
autonomous vehicle space, and it was not
entirely new to us. However, this was different. This wasn't about winning
a race in the desert. This was about proving that
autonomous vehicles can work on real roads and, if so,
redefine the future of driving. So when we started,
our first goal was to learn as much as
possible about the space. And to that end, Google's
founders, Larry and Sergey, gave our team two challenges. The first one was to drive
100,000 miles in autonomous mode. And at the time, this
was orders of magnitude more than anyone has done. And the second challenge, and
that one actually turned out
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
gave our team two challenges. The first one was to drive
100,000 miles in autonomous mode. And at the time, this
was orders of magnitude more than anyone has done. And the second challenge, and
that one actually turned out to be the more difficult
one, was to drive 10 routes. Each one was about
100 miles long. And we had to complete each
one, from beginning to end, with no human intervention. So we're super excited
when we managed to finish both of
these challenges in about a year and a half. So again, technology of 2009, a
team of about a dozen engineers, 18 months, and we got it done. However, there's a
massive difference between doing 10 routes once
or building a system that just works most of the
time versus building a reliable, scalable,
fully autonomous service with no human behind the wheel. And the challenge isn't
just building the tech. It's proving that it's safe. It's earning the public's trust. It's building a product that
people love and actually want to use. So as you can see, it's
been a long road for us. But we've always built for
the long game, layer by layer, never cutting any
corners or compromising on what really matters. And that's what got
us to where we are. And today, Waymo is operating
a 24/7 fully autonomous ride hailing service with no human
behind the wheel in four major US markets-- San Francisco, Phoenix,
LA, and Austin. [APPLAUSE] Thank you. And soon bringing it to
Atlanta, Miami, and DC and many more cities after that. Every week, we're serving more
than a quarter of a million fully autonomous paid trips,
and we're growing exponentially. This is five times higher
than the same time last year. In fact, we just hit
a major milestone. We crossed 10 million fully
autonomous paid trips. And half of those took
place just this year alone, in less than five months. This is what exponential
scaling looks like. [APPLAUSE] But we're just getting started. With every mile driven, we're
also making our roads safer. The data speaks for itself. When it comes to serious
collisions, anything involving
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
This is what exponential
scaling looks like. [APPLAUSE] But we're just getting started. With every mile driven, we're
also making our roads safer. The data speaks for itself. When it comes to serious
collisions, anything involving an injury, or
airbag deployments, the Waymo Driver is about
five times safer than humans. And for anything
involving pedestrians, the results are even stronger. Now, this data purely looks
at rates of collisions with no consideration
of who is at fault. If you take that into account,
the results are even stronger. A large insurance company, Swiss
Re, has analyzed the Waymo data and used insurance claim
as a proxy for fault, and they found the Waymo Driver
to be about 10 times better than humans. Now, this is a safety record
that we feel proud of. And with more than 50 million
fully autonomous miles under our belt at
this point, we feel like we're actually starting to
realize our mission of making our roads safer. Now, let me show you
a couple of examples of the types of
situations and behaviors by the Waymo Driver that lead
to these overall numbers. This first example
is from Austin. What you're seeing here
is a view from a car. Cameras on the bottom
are how the scene would appear to a human driver. And then at the top is how the
Waymo Driver sees the scene. So keep an eye on that scooter
rider up ahead and to our right. All of a sudden, they lose
their balance and stumble onto the road right
in front of us. The Waymo Driver
reacts immediately, swerves to the left,
keeping both the scooter rider and our passengers safe. [APPLAUSE] Here's another one. An oncoming truck
swerves into our lane. The Waymo Driver again
reacts immediately, nudges onto the shoulder,
avoids what could have been a head-on collision. So this Superhuman
performance is the result of the state-of-the-art AI that
is the backbone of the Waymo Driver. Now, there are a
few things that make autonomous driving a uniquely
challenging AI problem. There's three main
aspects of that.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
So this Superhuman
performance is the result of the state-of-the-art AI that
is the backbone of the Waymo Driver. Now, there are a
few things that make autonomous driving a uniquely
challenging AI problem. There's three main
aspects of that. The first one is that we operate
in a complex, messy, noisy physical environment. We have to deal with
different weather conditions-- rain, fog, dust
storms, and so forth. And we share the
roads with humans, who often act unpredictably
and sometimes recklessly. The second aspect is that it is
a safety-critical environment. So any mistake can have very
serious and very immediate consequences. That means our tolerance for
errors is very, very low. And we need a lot of
nines in our performance across the entire stack. And the last aspect is that
it's a real-time environment. Our AI doesn't run
on a data center somewhere where it
can take a few seconds to process all the data,
crunch the numbers, and come up with the
right driving decision. It all happens in real
time on board the vehicle, where milliseconds
matter, especially when we're moving at high speeds. And all of the hard problems
are in the long tail of driving, the rare events. It's things like a grill falling
off a truck in front of you on a freeway or trees
coming down during a storm, flooding, toddlers on the
loose, and so much more. In fact, every day
today, the Waymo Driver encountered situations
that many of us would never see in a
lifetime of driving. So how do we go about
tackling this problem? Well, because we operate
in the physical world, it starts with sensors. At Waymo, we use three
main sensing modalities-- LiDAR, cameras, and radars. Each one has a full 360-degree
coverage around the vehicle. And they have different
physical characteristics, so they complement
each other very nicely and give us redundancy. Here's what the data
actually looks like. Again, cameras on the bottom. And at the top is what we get
from our state-of-the-art, custom-designed LiDAR
and imaging radar.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
so they complement
each other very nicely and give us redundancy. Here's what the data
actually looks like. Again, cameras on the bottom. And at the top is what we get
from our state-of-the-art, custom-designed LiDAR
and imaging radar. So these yellowish-brown 3D
shapes is what we get from our LiDAR. And then this heat
map on the ground is what the world looks
like to our imaging radar. And working together,
these sensors can give the Waymo Driver
superhuman sensing ability. Let me show you an example. This is from Phoenix. We're driving in a dust storm. This is what the scene looks
like to our high-resolution, high-dynamic range camera,
not unlike what any of us would see if we
were to drive there. This is what it looks like to
our LiDAR for that same frame. You can much more
clearly see that there's a pedestrian standing
on the side of the road. So imagine they were
to step onto our path. This early detection can
make a really big difference in how the situation plays out. But as great as these sensors
are, they're just that, sensors. They give you raw,
sometimes noisy measurements of the world around you. So it all comes down to our AI
to interpret all of that data and make good driving decisions. In fact, AI and ML has played
a key role in our technology from the earliest days. And over the years,
we've seen multiple waves of innovation and
big breakthroughs that helped us improve
the Waymo Driver. And in many cases, we actually
pushed the field forward ourselves. One of the big
early breakthroughs was due to the advance of
convolutional neural nets, or ConvNets, which
took off about 2013. And they were a game
changer in computer vision, and they really gave us a
big boost on the perception side of things. Then, in 2017 came an even
more profound change-- transformers. This is the foundational
architecture that powers all of the
large language models that we see today. And for us, it gave us an
additional boost in perception. But even more importantly, it
led to some major breakthroughs
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
This is the foundational
architecture that powers all of the
large language models that we see today. And for us, it gave us an
additional boost in perception. But even more importantly, it
led to some major breakthroughs in the area of understanding
intent, making predictions about the behaviors of
others, and applying ML to this problem of decision
making and planning. It turns out that modeling the
interactive aspects of driving, the social aspects of driving,
is not unlike modeling a conversation, except
instead of words, you have states of objects. And instead of
sentences, you have trajectories of those objects. And then, most recently,
large language models and visual language
models, LLMs and VLMs, have opened up entirely
new possibilities for us that allow us to combine
the expertise of the Waymo AI with the general world
knowledge of VLMs. So how can we use VLMs
for autonomous driving? Well, one use case
is just understanding language, understanding text. Take this parking
sign, for example. It's a little scary,
a little daunting. By the time you're done
reading it and decoding it, you're probably
getting towed already. So we asked Gemini. It's Wednesday at 4:00 PM. Can you park here? Anybody? Yes, no? Well, Gemini comes back with
the correct answer right away. Another area where using VLMs
is very helpful for autonomous driving is just general
scene understanding. For example, you can
give this image to Gemini and ask it to interpret
what's going on and what driving actions
one should take here. And it comes back right away
with a deep understanding of the context of
the scene, as well as some high-level description
of appropriate actions. So naturally, the next step is
to go for deeper integration. And last year, we
published some research that we called EMMA
that does just that. Essentially, it is an
end-to-end ML model that takes in input from
multiple cameras and, as output, produces a trajectory. And almost out of the box,
it drives reasonably well. Now, this is a research model.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
Essentially, it is an
end-to-end ML model that takes in input from
multiple cameras and, as output, produces a trajectory. And almost out of the box,
it drives reasonably well. Now, this is a research model. It has certain limitations. It's not safe enough to drive on
its own without a human driver. But it illustrates
the huge potential of large multi-modal language
models for autonomous driving. So to leverage that
potential, we've been developing what we call the
Waymo Foundation Model, which combines the AV domain expertise
of the Waymo AI backbone and the general world
knowledge of VLMs. It's essentially an
encoder and decoder model. The job of the encoder, or the
perception side on the left, is to take the data from
all of our sensors-- the LiDARs, the
cameras, the radars-- and to compress it or encode
it into a representation that contains all of the
information that's relevant for the
task of driving. And the decoder, the
part on the right, that's the generative part. That's the part that understands
the social interactive aspects of driving that
can model behavior. It can predict what
other people will do. It can generate trajectories
and driving behaviors. It's also the foundation
for generative models of agent behavior that
we use for simulation. And that brings me to the second
key challenge of autonomous driving, just beyond the
building of a good driver, and that's evaluation. Now, key to good evaluation
is a good simulator. So what makes a good simulator? Well, first of
all, it needs to be able to run at very large scale,
simulating billions of miles. Second, it needs to be
able to run in closed loop, meaning that if you take an
action in this simulated world, the world responds
appropriately. And third, it needs
to be realistic, both from the perspective
of sensor realism as well as from the perspective
of behavioral realism, meaning that all of the
simulated agents, all the simulated road users,
are behaving the way they would in real life. So we have a bit of an
interesting chicken and egg
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
as well as from the perspective
of behavioral realism, meaning that all of the
simulated agents, all the simulated road users,
are behaving the way they would in real life. So we have a bit of an
interesting chicken and egg problem. To build a good driver, you
need to build a good simulator. And to build a
good simulator, you need to create realistic models
of pedestrians, cyclists, and other vehicles. So in many ways, these
two key technical pillars of the AV problem actually share
the same technical foundation. And we've made some really
great strides in the Waymo AI to power both. So let me show you a couple
of examples of the work that we have been
doing in simulation. Here's one example where
we created a dense 3D reconstruction of a block of
San Francisco at scale using millions of camera images. And once we have that
[INAUDIBLE] model, then, in simulation, we
can generate all kinds of different versions
by varying the time of day or weather and other
environmental conditions. And we've pushed
this line of work further and used
diffusion techniques that are commonly used in
[? video ?] generation to give us fully controllable
models, where not only can we control the global environmental
properties of the scene, as here, but we can also add
or remove individual objects, allowing us to
generate and simulate a whole number of new
dynamic traffic situations for the purpose of
training, evaluating, and testing the Waymo Driver. Now, all of these components-- sensing, AI, simulation,
and evaluation-- come together to
give the Waymo Driver truly superhuman performance. Let me show you an example. This is from San Francisco. It's a fairly nominal situation,
where, waiting at a red light, there's a bus that
crosses in front of us. So let's see how this goes. Our light turns green. We proceed. And notice, we are
detecting a pedestrian on the other side of the bus. That would be completely
occluded to a human driver. So what's happening
here is that our sensors are able to pick up the
movement of the person's feet
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
And notice, we are
detecting a pedestrian on the other side of the bus. That would be completely
occluded to a human driver. So what's happening
here is that our sensors are able to pick up the
movement of the person's feet under the bus. And just that little bit
of noisy and sparse signal is enough for the
Waymo Driver to detect that there's a pedestrian there
and, furthermore, to predict what they're going
to do in the future, allowing us to take a
defensive action early, keeping everyone safe. So all of these AI
advances have tremendously accelerated our progress,
making the Waymo Driver truly generalizable. Here are all the
places where we've tested over the
last couple of years beyond our mature
commercial markets. It includes everything
from snowy Upstate New York to the left-hand side
traffic in Tokyo. And with the help of
AI, the Waymo Driver is learning fast
and adapting quickly to all of these
different environments. And not only is the Waymo
Driver generalizing well across different geographies,
it is generalizing well across different
vehicle platforms and across different
applications. Today, we're very
focused on ride hailing. In the future, the
Waymo Driver will power all kinds of
products, all kinds of commercial applications,
from local deliveries, to long-haul trucking,
to personal vehicles. By 2030, total
vehicle miles traveled is projected to
reach 20 trillion. Just imagine the impact
that the Waymo Driver will have taking all of
these opportunities together. But across every use case, one
thing remains constant for us, and that's trust. It's core to what we have
built, and it always will be. And trust doesn't
just happen overnight. Trust is earned, with
every mile we drive, with every ride we serve,
with every bit of technology that we deployed. And the thing that makes us
happy is our happy riders. So let's hear from
them directly. [VIDEO PLAYBACK] [MUSIC PLAYING] - What are we doing? - We're in our first ever Waymo. - And what does it mean
when we're in a Waymo?
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
And the thing that makes us
happy is our happy riders. So let's hear from
them directly. [VIDEO PLAYBACK] [MUSIC PLAYING] - What are we doing? - We're in our first ever Waymo. - And what does it mean
when we're in a Waymo? - It means that there
is nobody driving. - There's nobody [BLEEP]
driving this thing. - And this is a fully
autonomous Waymo ride. - The truck was over
the yellow line, so the Waymo braked
and moved to the side. - It knew how to
pronounce my name. - Oh my god. Look at this. - Wow. It's nice. I love it. This is nuts. - Ah! - This is so cool. [END PLAYBACK] ADITI ROY: Makes me want to
go into a Waymo right now. [APPLAUSE] Thank you so much,
Dmitri, for that look inside this
fascinating technology. A reminder to the audience,
have your questions prepared. You can start lining
up on either side of the stage at the mics. While you think
of your questions, I have a couple of my
own to start us off. You spoke about
how AI has really turbocharged the acceleration
into an expansion to new cities. Can you go into more detail
about how things are different now compared to five years ago? DMITRI DOLGOV: Let me start with
the second part of the question, because five years ago, things
were qualitatively different. Actually, five
years ago, in 2020, we had just begun to offer
our fully autonomous service to the public. And it was in the East
Valley of Phoenix, in a city called Chandler. But that was on the fourth
generation of the Waymo Driver, the previous generation. And while we were
with that deployment, we were learning all the million
things that it takes to actually operate a 24/7
commercial service. On the technical side,
we were doubling down on making a leap from
the fourth generation to the fifth generation. And the fifth generation is
what we see on the roads today. And by far the biggest delta
from between those generations was our aggressive
big bet on AI. And that really paid off with
all of the safety performance that we talked about
on the pace of scaling that we're seeing today.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
And by far the biggest delta
from between those generations was our aggressive
big bet on AI. And that really paid off with
all of the safety performance that we talked about
on the pace of scaling that we're seeing today. Now, in terms of
the specific role that AI plays in
generalizability and going to different cities and going
to different geographies, I would say that
I primarily don't look at it from the perspective
of cities or zip codes. We primarily focus
on the capabilities of the Waymo Driver. So we have this term,
the operating domain or operating design domain. It's basically the set of all of
the features in the environment that we deem the Waymo Driver to
be fully ready for for a fully autonomous operation. So the-- now, if you look
at it through that lens, there's cities, and
then there's cities. Given where we are today,
look at the operating domain and all the capabilities of the
Waymo Driver across weather, across different cities, when we
are operating in San Francisco, LA, Phoenix, Austin. There's many cities that
fall, today, kind of squarely into that operating
domain where we already have robust performance and
confidence in the Waymo Driver. Take, for example, Atlanta. And those cities,
it's all a matter of validation and confirmation. And we find that we show up, and
it kind of works out of the box right away. And then there's other
cities, like Tokyo, where we do need to expand
the operating domain. And we do need to
teach the Waymo Driver, for example, to drive on
the other side of the road, or maybe the traffic
lights or the signs are a little bit different. And there we're finding
that the big bet on AI is really paying off, because
you just give it new data, and it learns incredibly
well and incredibly fast. And if you think about
it, it's actually not that counterintuitive,
because if you show up to a new city, what are the
hardest problems in autonomous driving? Well, it's not detecting the
signs and the different colored cones. It is the fundamental
understanding.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
that counterintuitive,
because if you show up to a new city, what are the
hardest problems in autonomous driving? Well, it's not detecting the
signs and the different colored cones. It is the fundamental
understanding. So when you go up and
show up to a new place, pedestrians still look the same. Cars largely look the same. How people interact with
one another fundamentally remains the same. So that's what we're seeing,
and it's very encouraging. And with time, the operating
domain will just expand, and the speed of
iteration, with all of the amazing progress in AI,
will just get faster and faster. ADITI ROY: What will
be some of the most profound positive impacts of
autonomous driving to society? DMITRI DOLGOV: Well, for
the most profound impact, I immediately go back to safety. I talked about it-- again,
million lives lost on the roads across the world every
year, in this country alone, about 40,000. This is insane. Even if we invented
cars today, there's no way we'd be OK with that. But it goes beyond that. I think not only will
autonomous vehicles save lives, they will change our
lives in many ways. They will change
our relationship with time, how we experience
cities, and so much more. So in terms of time,
today, an average American spends about 300 hours a year
sitting behind the wheel. For the whole country, that's
about 80 billion hours. Think about all the wonderful
things we can do with that time. As the deployments and
the autonomous vehicles scale up across our
cities, across countries, I think the cities themselves
will start to change. The streets will
change, the parking lots will shrink, and so forth. Another thing that
I find, actually, that's maybe the one that
is closest to my heart and to many people at
Waymo, beyond safety, is accessibility and making
transportation inclusive. I mean, it should be
inclusive, but today it isn't. People who are blind,
people who have low vision, sometimes they have
trouble finding the ride in a busy pickup area.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – jnUUo7xso_0
text:
is accessibility and making
transportation inclusive. I mean, it should be
inclusive, but today it isn't. People who are blind,
people who have low vision, sometimes they have
trouble finding the ride in a busy pickup area. Sometimes they just get rejected
because of their guide dogs or they just don't feel safe. With Waymo, we've designed
everything from the ground up, from beginning to end, to
be inclusive and accessible. And it can be very powerful. Many things for us,
we get our time back. That's nice to have. For many people
with disabilities, it's really life changing. There's a couple that I
recently learned about. It's a married couple. They are blind. They live in Phoenix. They have not been
in a car together alone for more than 30 years. And today they use Waymo. They love it. It gives them the
freedom of mobility without needing to
rely on other people. So that can be life
changing for many people. And I guess maybe the
last thing I'll say is that today, with the
trillions of miles that we're driving and only
increasing, there are so many aspects of
society that rely on that foundational
layer of moving people and goods around that
once there's autonomy across that stack,
I think we'll see all kinds of new applications,
all kinds of new benefits that pop up over time. ADITI ROY: So many
positive repercussions. Dmitri Dolgov, thank you
so much for your time. DMITRI DOLGOV: Thank you. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
